cards.definition: a change in behaviour or beliefs due to real or imagined group pressure
conforming publicly without genuinely changing private views — the shallowest type
conforming both publicly and privately — genuinely accepting group norms
conforming to the views of a group you value — temporary, associated with the role
participants gave obviously wrong answers to match the majority — 75% conformed at least once
conforming to fit in and be liked — based on the need for social approval
conforming because you believe others know better — especially in ambiguous situations
65% of participants administered what they believed were lethal electric shocks when ordered by an authority figure
Milgram — in an 'agentic state', people see themselves as agents of authority — moral responsibility transferred to the authority
the presence of a dissenter reduces conformity and obedience — empowers individuals to resist

questions.question: What is conformity?
options: A) Changing behaviour due to direct instruction
B) Changing behaviour or beliefs due to group pressure
C) Obeying an authority figure
D) Agreeing with a majority for personal gain
questions.explanation: Conformity is yielding to group pressure — it can be public only (compliance) or both public and private (internalisation).

questions.question: What did Asch's study find?
options: A) Nobody conformed to wrong answers
B) Only 5% conformed
C) 75% of participants conformed at least once to obviously wrong answers
D) All participants refused to conform
questions.explanation: Asch's line study: 75% conformed at least once. 5% always conformed. Only 25% never conformed — showing the powerful pull of majority influence.

questions.question: What is normative social influence?
options: A) Conforming because others know better
B) Conforming to fit in and gain social approval
C) Obeying an authority figure
D) Conforming in ambiguous situations
questions.explanation: NSI is driven by the need to be liked and accepted — we conform to avoid rejection. Most powerful in social situations where belonging matters.

questions.question: What did Milgram find?
options: A) Nobody administered shocks
B) Only 5% obeyed
C) 65% administered what they believed were lethal shocks when ordered
D) All participants refused after 150V
questions.explanation: Milgram found 65% of participants continued to the maximum 450V — demonstrating the power of authority over individual conscience.

questions.question: What is agency theory?
options: A) People are naturally aggressive
B) In an agentic state, people see themselves as agents of authority — transferring moral responsibility
C) Conformity is always harmful
D) People resist authority when they have social support
questions.explanation: Agency theory: people shift from autonomous state (own conscience) to agentic state (following orders) — they feel responsibility belongs to the authority.

questions.question: What is internalisation?
options: A) Conforming publicly without changing private beliefs
B) Conforming both publicly and privately — genuinely accepting the group's view
C) Conforming to a valued group role
D) Obeying an authority figure
questions.explanation: Internalisation is the deepest form of conformity — the individual genuinely adopts the group's beliefs as their own.

questions.question: What is informational social influence?
options: A) Conforming for social approval
B) Conforming because you believe others have more knowledge — especially in ambiguous situations
C) Conforming to an authority figure
D) Conforming in familiar situations
questions.explanation: ISI occurs when we look to others for information — especially in novel or unclear situations. We assume they know better.

questions.question: What is social support in the context of obedience?
options: A) Having friends nearby during an experiment
B) The presence of a dissenter who empowers others to resist
C) Being supported by authority
D) Conforming to support others
questions.explanation: When a dissenter disobeys, obedience rates in Milgram's study dropped dramatically — seeing someone else resist makes it easier to do the same.

questions.question: What is compliance?
options: A) Obeying an authority figure
B) Conforming both publicly and privately
C) Conforming publicly without genuinely changing private views
D) Refusing to conform
questions.explanation: Compliance is the shallowest form of conformity — going along with the group publicly while privately disagreeing.

questions.question: What is identification in conformity?
options: A) Identifying a deviant in a group
B) Conforming to the views of a valued group — associated with the role, not permanent
C) Deep, permanent change in private beliefs
D) Refusing to obey an authority
questions.explanation: Identification — e.g. adopting a nurse's uniform values while training — is temporary. When the role ends, the conformity may cease.

questions.question: Internalisation is the shallowest form of conformity.
questions.explanation: Compliance is the shallowest form. Internalisation is the deepest — genuine private acceptance of group norms.

questions.question: Milgram found 65% of participants continued to the maximum 450V.
questions.explanation: 65% gave what they believed were lethal shocks — demonstrating the power of legitimate authority.

questions.question: Normative social influence is based on wanting to be liked.
questions.explanation: NSI is driven by the need for social acceptance — we conform to avoid rejection and gain approval.

questions.question: Asch's study showed that nobody conforms to obviously wrong answers.
questions.explanation: 75% of participants conformed at least once — only 25% never conformed at all.

questions.question: In agency theory, the agentic state means acting on your own conscience.
questions.explanation: In the AGENTIC state, people act as agents of authority and transfer responsibility to the authority. Acting on your own conscience is the AUTONOMOUS state.

questions.question: Informational social influence is most powerful in familiar situations.
questions.explanation: ISI is most powerful in AMBIGUOUS or NEW situations — when people are uncertain, they look to others for information.

questions.question: Social support from a dissenter reduces obedience.
questions.explanation: When Milgram introduced a disobedient confederate, obedience dropped from 65% to around 10%.

questions.question: Compliance involves genuinely changing private beliefs.
questions.explanation: Compliance is only public conformity — private beliefs remain unchanged. Internalisation involves genuinely adopting new beliefs.

questions.question: Identification is a permanent form of conformity.
questions.explanation: Identification is temporary — it lasts while the person values the group or holds the role. It is not as deep as internalisation.

questions.question: Asch's study demonstrates the power of majority influence.
questions.explanation: Even when the majority was clearly wrong, participants often agreed — showing how powerful group pressure is.

questions.question: ________ is a change in behaviour or beliefs due to real or imagined group pressure.
questions.explanation: Conformity can be compliance (public only), identification (role-based), or internalisation (genuine).

questions.question: ________ is conforming publicly without genuinely changing private views — the shallowest form.
questions.explanation: Compliance is surface-level — the person agrees outwardly but privately disagrees.

questions.question: ________ is conforming both publicly and privately — genuinely accepting group norms.
questions.explanation: Internalisation is the deepest form of conformity — the belief becomes the person's own.

questions.question: ________ social influence is conforming to fit in and be liked.
questions.explanation: NSI is driven by the need for social approval and fear of rejection.

questions.question: ________ social influence is conforming because others seem to know better.
questions.explanation: ISI is most powerful in ambiguous situations where we are uncertain about the correct response.

questions.question: Milgram found ________ % of participants administered what they believed were lethal shocks.
questions.explanation: This shocking finding demonstrated the power of legitimate authority over individual conscience.

questions.question: Asch found ________ % of participants conformed at least once to obviously wrong answers.
questions.explanation: Only 25% of participants never conformed — showing the extraordinary power of majority influence.

questions.question: ________ theory: in an agentic state, people transfer moral responsibility to an authority figure.
questions.explanation: The shift from autonomous to agentic state explains obedience — people feel they are 'just following orders'.

questions.question: The presence of a ________ reduces conformity and obedience by empowering resistance.
questions.explanation: When someone else resists, it becomes easier to resist yourself — social support is powerful.

questions.question: ________ is conforming to the views of a valued group — temporary and role-related.
questions.explanation: E.g. a new nurse adopts medical values — but these may change when the role ends.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: public conformity without private change — shallowest
public and private conformity — deepest, genuine
conforming to fit in and be liked
conforming because others seem to know better
agentic state — moral responsibility transferred to authority

cards.definition: Atkinson & Shiffrin — sensory register → short-term memory → long-term memory. Information passes through stores with rehearsal
the first store — holds all sensory information briefly (< 1 second); most is lost unless attended to
limited capacity (7±2 items), short duration (15–30 seconds), encoded acoustically (by sound)
unlimited capacity, potentially permanent duration, encoded semantically (by meaning)
the repetition of information to transfer it from STM to LTM in the multi-store model
Baddeley & Hitch — STM has multiple components: central executive, phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, episodic buffer
the 'boss' of working memory — coordinates the slave systems and allocates attention
processes and stores verbal/acoustic information — includes the phonological store and articulatory process
processes and stores visual and spatial information — the 'inner eye'
a temporary store integrating information from the other components and LTM

questions.question: What are the three stores in the multi-store model?
options: A) Sensory, working, long-term
B) Sensory register, short-term memory, long-term memory
C) STM, LTM, rehearsal
D) Iconic, echoic, semantic
questions.explanation: Atkinson & Shiffrin's multi-store model: information flows from sensory register → STM → LTM through attention and rehearsal.

questions.question: What is the capacity of short-term memory?
options: A) Unlimited
B) 1–3 items
C) 7±2 items
D) 100+ items
questions.explanation: Miller's magic number: STM can hold 7 (plus or minus 2) items. Chunking can increase this.

questions.question: How is information encoded in long-term memory?
options: A) Acoustically (by sound)
B) Visually (by sight)
C) Semantically (by meaning)
D) Procedurally (by action
questions.explanation: LTM is mainly encoded semantically — by the meaning of information. STM uses mainly acoustic encoding.

questions.question: What is the central executive in working memory?
options: A) A phonological store
B) The boss of working memory — allocates attention and coordinates slave systems
C) A visual processing unit
D) A rehearsal loop
questions.explanation: The central executive is the most important component — it directs attention and coordinates the phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad.

questions.question: What is the phonological loop?
options: A) A visual processing system
B) A temporary store for spatial information
C) A component processing and storing verbal/acoustic information
D) The central attention controller
questions.explanation: The phonological loop has two parts: the phonological store (holds sound-based information) and the articulatory process (the 'inner voice' that rehearses information).

questions.question: What is the visuospatial sketchpad?
options: A) Processes verbal information
B) Coordinates working memory
C) Processes and stores visual and spatial information
D) Integrates information from LTM
questions.explanation: The visuospatial sketchpad (the 'inner eye') processes visual images and spatial relationships — e.g. imagining a route.

questions.question: What is the role of rehearsal in the multi-store model?
options: A) It retrieves information from LTM
B) It transfers information from STM to LTM
C) It encodes information semantically
D) It stores information in the sensory register
questions.explanation: In the multi-store model, maintenance rehearsal transfers information from STM to LTM by repetition — without rehearsal, information decays from STM.

questions.question: What is the episodic buffer?
options: A) The boss of working memory
B) A visual spatial store
C) A temporary store integrating information from other WMM components and LTM
D) The long-term memory store
questions.explanation: Baddeley added the episodic buffer later — it is a temporary, limited-capacity store that integrates information from the phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, and LTM.

questions.question: What is the duration of short-term memory without rehearsal?
options: A) Unlimited
B) 30 minutes
C) 15–30 seconds
D) 1 minute
questions.explanation: Peterson & Peterson found STM duration is 15–30 seconds without rehearsal — information rapidly decays.

questions.question: What is the capacity of long-term memory?
options: A) 7±2 items
B) Limited to recent memories
C) Unlimited
D) Around 100 items
questions.explanation: LTM has unlimited capacity and potentially permanent duration — though retrieval may fail.

questions.question: The multi-store model was proposed by Atkinson and Shiffrin.
questions.explanation: Atkinson & Shiffrin (1968) proposed the three-store model: sensory register → STM → LTM.

questions.question: Short-term memory has unlimited capacity.
questions.explanation: STM has limited capacity — 7±2 items (Miller). LTM has unlimited capacity.

questions.question: LTM is encoded mainly acoustically.
questions.explanation: LTM is encoded mainly semantically (by meaning). STM is encoded mainly acoustically (by sound).

questions.question: The central executive is a slave system in the working memory model.
questions.explanation: The central executive is the BOSS — it controls the slave systems (phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad).

questions.question: The phonological loop processes visual and spatial information.
questions.explanation: The phonological loop processes VERBAL/ACOUSTIC information. The visuospatial sketchpad processes visual and spatial information.

questions.question: Rehearsal transfers information from STM to LTM in the multi-store model.
questions.explanation: Without rehearsal, information in STM decays within 15–30 seconds.

questions.question: The visuospatial sketchpad is sometimes called the inner eye.
questions.explanation: The visuospatial sketchpad processes visual images and spatial information — like an inner eye.

questions.question: The episodic buffer integrates information from all working memory components and LTM.
questions.explanation: The episodic buffer links working memory to LTM — it was added by Baddeley in 2000.

questions.question: STM duration without rehearsal is about 15–30 seconds.
questions.explanation: Peterson & Peterson demonstrated rapid decay of STM without rehearsal.

questions.question: LTM has limited duration, lasting about one week.
questions.explanation: LTM has potentially permanent duration — though retrieval can fail. There is no inherent time limit.

questions.question: The multi-store model has three stores: sensory register, ________, and LTM.
questions.explanation: Atkinson & Shiffrin proposed information flows through these stores via attention and rehearsal.

questions.question: STM has a capacity of ________ (±2) items.
questions.explanation: Miller's magic number — chunking can increase effective capacity by grouping items.

questions.question: STM is encoded mainly ________ (by sound).
questions.explanation: Conrad showed STM confuses acoustically similar letters — evidence for acoustic encoding.

questions.question: LTM is encoded mainly ________ (by meaning).
questions.explanation: Baddeley showed LTM confuses semantically similar words — evidence for semantic encoding.

questions.question: In the working memory model, the ________ executive coordinates all other components.
questions.explanation: The central executive is the most important but least understood component of working memory.

questions.question: The ________ loop processes and stores verbal and acoustic information.
questions.explanation: The phonological loop has the phonological store and articulatory process (inner voice).

questions.question: The ________ sketchpad processes visual and spatial information.
questions.explanation: Also called the 'inner eye' — it handles imagery and spatial reasoning.

questions.question: The ________ buffer integrates information from WMM components and LTM.
questions.explanation: Baddeley added the episodic buffer in 2000 to explain how WMM links to LTM.

questions.question: Without ________, information in STM decays within 15–30 seconds.
questions.explanation: Rehearsal maintains information in STM and can transfer it to LTM.

questions.question: LTM has ________ capacity and potentially permanent duration.
questions.explanation: Unlike STM, LTM appears to have no limits on how much can be stored.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: sensory register → STM → LTM via attention and rehearsal
capacity 7±2, duration 15–30s, acoustic encoding
unlimited capacity, permanent, semantic encoding
boss of working memory — allocates attention
processes verbal/acoustic information

cards.definition: defining abnormality as behaviour that is rare or statistically unusual — e.g. an IQ below 70
behaviour that violates the implicit rules of society — what is 'normal' varies by culture and era
abnormality defined as inability to maintain normal day-to-day functioning — Rosenhan & Seligman criteria
Jahoda — abnormality is the absence of positive mental health criteria, e.g. self-actualisation, autonomy
a talking therapy targeting faulty thinking patterns (cognitions) and their effects on behaviour
a behavioural therapy for phobias — gradual exposure to feared stimuli while maintaining relaxation
a behavioural therapy — immediate, prolonged exposure to a feared stimulus — extinction of fear response
Freud — treating mental illness by uncovering unconscious conflicts through techniques like free association
using medication to treat mental illness — e.g. SSRIs for depression, antipsychotics for schizophrenia
explains abnormality as learnt behaviour — phobias acquired through classical conditioning

questions.question: What is statistical infrequency as a definition of abnormality?
options: A) Any behaviour that breaks social rules
B) Behaviour that is statistically rare or unusual
C) Failure to cope with daily life
D) Absence of positive mental health
questions.explanation: Statistical infrequency defines abnormality as anything rare — e.g. IQ below 70 or above 130. Problem: some rare behaviours are desirable (high IQ).

questions.question: What is CBT?
options: A) A drug treatment for anxiety
B) A talking therapy targeting faulty thinking and its effect on behaviour
C) Gradual exposure to feared stimuli
D) Psychoanalytic treatment of unconscious conflict
questions.explanation: CBT identifies and challenges negative automatic thoughts — replacing faulty cognitions with more realistic thinking patterns.

questions.question: What is systematic desensitisation?
options: A) Immediate flooding with the feared stimulus
B) A drug therapy for phobias
C) Gradual exposure to feared stimuli combined with relaxation
D) Exploring unconscious fears through hypnosis
questions.explanation: SD works by pairing the feared stimulus with relaxation — gradually working up a hierarchy of anxiety from least to most feared.

questions.question: What is the behavioural explanation of phobias?
options: A) Phobias are caused by unconscious conflict
B) Phobias are irrational thought patterns
C) Phobias are learnt through classical conditioning — e.g. a frightening experience with a dog
D) Phobias are inherited genetically
questions.explanation: The two-process model (Mowrer): phobias are acquired through classical conditioning and maintained through operant conditioning (avoidance reduces fear).

questions.question: What is deviation from social norms?
options: A) Statistically rare behaviour
B) Behaviour violating the implicit social rules of society
C) Failure to cope with daily tasks
D) Absence of self-actualisation
questions.explanation: Deviation from social norms defines abnormality as violating unwritten rules — but what is normal varies by culture and time period.

questions.question: What is flooding as a therapy?
options: A) Gradual, calm exposure to a feared stimulus
B) Immediate, prolonged exposure to a feared stimulus until anxiety reduces
C) Using medication to reduce anxiety
D) Talking through fears in a therapeutic setting
questions.explanation: Flooding exposes patients to the maximum feared stimulus immediately — anxiety peaks then decreases (extinction) as the CS-fear association breaks down.

questions.question: What is drug therapy in psychology?
options: A) A type of talking therapy
B) Behavioural techniques to change abnormal behaviour
C) Medication to treat mental illness — e.g. SSRIs for depression
D) Exposure therapy for phobias
questions.explanation: Drug therapies target neurochemical imbalances — SSRIs increase serotonin for depression; antipsychotics reduce dopamine for schizophrenia.

questions.question: What is 'failure to function adequately'?
options: A) Having an IQ below 70
B) Breaking social norms
C) Inability to maintain normal day-to-day functioning
D) Absence of positive mental health criteria
questions.explanation: Rosenhan & Seligman proposed criteria including: personal distress, unpredictability, maladaptive behaviour, and inability to function normally.

questions.question: What is Jahoda's deviation from ideal mental health?
options: A) Statistical rarity
B) Breaking social norms
C) Absence of positive health criteria — e.g. self-actualisation, autonomy, accurate self-perception
D) Failure to maintain relationships
questions.explanation: Jahoda defined abnormality as the absence of positive mental health — including criteria like self-actualisation, autonomy, and resistance to stress.

questions.question: What is the main aim of psychoanalysis?
options: A) To change faulty thinking patterns
B) To gradually expose patients to feared stimuli
C) To uncover and resolve unconscious conflicts
D) To alter neurochemical imbalances
questions.explanation: Psychoanalysis (Freud) uses techniques like free association, dream analysis, and transference to bring unconscious conflicts into consciousness and resolve them.

questions.question: Statistical infrequency means any unusual behaviour is abnormal.
questions.explanation: Statistical infrequency defines abnormality as rare behaviour — but some rare behaviours are desirable (e.g. exceptional talent).

questions.question: CBT targets faulty thinking patterns and their effect on behaviour.
questions.explanation: CBT identifies negative automatic thoughts and challenges them — replacing faulty cognitions with realistic alternatives.

questions.question: Systematic desensitisation involves immediate, prolonged exposure to a feared stimulus.
questions.explanation: Systematic desensitisation uses GRADUAL exposure combined with relaxation. FLOODING involves immediate, prolonged exposure.

questions.question: Phobias are explained by the behavioural approach as learnt through classical conditioning.
questions.explanation: The two-process model: phobias are acquired via classical conditioning and maintained via operant conditioning (avoidance).

questions.question: Deviation from social norms is the same across all cultures.
questions.explanation: What counts as 'normal' varies by culture and historical era — this is a significant limitation of this definition.

questions.question: Drug therapy targets neurochemical imbalances in the brain.
questions.explanation: E.g. SSRIs increase serotonin availability for depression; antipsychotics reduce excess dopamine in schizophrenia.

questions.question: Failure to function adequately focuses on statistical rarity.
questions.explanation: Failure to function adequately focuses on inability to maintain normal daily life — personal distress, maladaptive behaviour, etc.

questions.question: Psychoanalysis aims to uncover unconscious conflicts.
questions.explanation: Freudian psychoanalysis uses free association and dream analysis to bring unconscious material to conscious awareness.

questions.question: Jahoda's ideal mental health requires the absence of all problems.
questions.explanation: Jahoda's criteria are POSITIVE — self-actualisation, autonomy, accurate perception, resistance to stress. It is the ABSENCE of these that defines abnormality.

questions.question: Flooding extinguishes fear through prolonged exposure — anxiety peaks then reduces.
questions.explanation: During flooding, the conditioned anxiety response peaks then extinguishes as the patient realises the stimulus is harmless.

questions.question: ________ infrequency defines abnormality as statistically rare behaviour.
questions.explanation: Problem: some rare traits are positive (genius) and some common ones are undesirable (depression).

questions.question: ________ from social norms defines abnormality as violating society's implicit rules.
questions.explanation: What is considered normal varies by culture and era — a key limitation.

questions.question: ________ to function adequately: inability to maintain normal day-to-day activities.
questions.explanation: Criteria include personal distress, unpredictability, and maladaptive behaviour.

questions.question: Jahoda's deviation from ________ mental health: abnormality as absence of positive criteria.
questions.explanation: Positive criteria include self-actualisation, autonomy, and accurate self-perception.

questions.question: ________ therapy targets faulty thinking and its effect on behaviour.
questions.explanation: CBT is used for depression, anxiety, and phobias — challenging negative automatic thoughts.

questions.question: Systematic ________ pairs gradual exposure to feared stimuli with relaxation.
questions.explanation: Starting with least feared stimuli, patients work up an anxiety hierarchy.

questions.question: ________ exposes patients immediately to the maximum feared stimulus.
questions.explanation: Prolonged exposure causes anxiety to peak then extinguish — the fear response is removed.

questions.question: ________ therapy uses medication — e.g. SSRIs for depression.
questions.explanation: SSRIs increase serotonin availability, improving mood in depression.

questions.question: ________ analysis uncovers unconscious conflicts through free association and dream analysis.
questions.explanation: Psychoanalysis is Freud's approach — bringing repressed material to conscious awareness.

questions.question: The ________ approach explains phobias as learnt through classical conditioning.
questions.explanation: Classical conditioning: neutral stimulus paired with frightening event → becomes conditioned fear stimulus.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: targets faulty thinking patterns — replaces with realistic cognitions
gradual exposure + relaxation — treats phobias
immediate prolonged exposure — extinguishes fear
abnormality = statistically rare behaviour
abnormality = violating society's implicit rules

cards.definition: a clear, testable prediction of what is expected to happen in a study
the variable manipulated by the researcher to see its effect
the variable measured by the researcher — what is affected by the IV
any variable other than the IV that might affect the DV — must be controlled
defining variables in a measurable way — e.g. 'stress' operationalised as 'heart rate'
takes place in a controlled environment — high internal validity but low ecological validity
takes place in a natural setting — higher ecological validity but less control
IV is naturally occurring rather than manipulated — researcher cannot randomly allocate participants
a measure of the relationship between two variables — does not establish causation
rules protecting participants — informed consent, deception, right to withdraw, confidentiality, debriefing

questions.question: What is an independent variable?
options: A) The variable being measured
B) The variable manipulated by the researcher
C) Any uncontrolled variable
D) The hypothesis being tested
questions.explanation: The IV is what the researcher changes. The DV is what changes as a result. E.g. IV = amount of sleep, DV = reaction time.

questions.question: What is operationalisation?
options: A) Writing a hypothesis
B) Defining variables in a measurable, testable way
C) Choosing which participants to study
D) Conducting an experiment in a laboratory
questions.explanation: Operationalisation makes abstract concepts measurable — e.g. 'aggression' operationalised as 'number of times a child hits another per hour'.

questions.question: What is a strength of laboratory experiments?
options: A) High ecological validity
B) High mundane realism
C) High internal validity — good control over extraneous variables
D) Cannot be replicated
questions.explanation: Lab experiments offer tight control — allowing cause-and-effect conclusions. Weakness: artificial setting reduces ecological validity.

questions.question: What is a correlation?
options: A) An experiment showing cause and effect
B) A measure of the relationship between two variables — not causation
C) A research method using interviews
D) A type of experimental design
questions.explanation: Correlation shows whether two variables are related (positive, negative, or zero) — but cannot establish which causes the other.

questions.question: What is a field experiment?
options: A) An experiment in a controlled lab
B) An experiment in a natural setting with IV manipulation
C) Naturally occurring IV with no manipulation
D) A questionnaire study
questions.explanation: Field experiments manipulate the IV in a real-world setting — higher ecological validity than lab experiments but less control over extraneous variables.

questions.question: What are ethical guidelines designed to protect?
options: A) The researcher's reputation
B) The validity of findings
C) Participants from harm — including psychological harm
D) The confidentiality of data only
questions.explanation: BPS ethical guidelines protect participants — covering informed consent, deception, right to withdraw, protection from harm, confidentiality, and debriefing.

questions.question: What is a natural experiment?
options: A) An experiment conducted outdoors
B) An experiment where the IV occurs naturally — researcher does not manipulate it
C) A correlational study
D) An experiment with no hypothesis
questions.explanation: Natural experiments use naturally occurring variations in the IV — e.g. studying effects of a natural disaster on stress. Higher ecological validity but cannot establish causation.

questions.question: What is the dependent variable?
options: A) The variable manipulated by the researcher
B) Any variable that interferes with results
C) The variable measured to assess the effect of the IV
D) The baseline measure before the experiment
questions.explanation: The DV is what is measured — it is expected to change as a result of the IV. E.g. if IV = caffeine dose, DV = reaction time.

questions.question: What is an extraneous variable?
options: A) The variable being measured
B) The variable being manipulated
C) Any variable other than the IV that could affect the DV
D) A variable deliberately introduced by the researcher
questions.explanation: Extraneous variables are controlled in well-designed studies — if they systematically affect results they become confounding variables.

questions.question: What is the right to withdraw?
options: A) Researchers can stop the study early
B) Participants can leave a study at any time without penalty
C) Participants must complete the full study
D) Researchers can withhold some data
questions.explanation: The right to withdraw is a key ethical guideline — participants must be free to leave at any time without pressure or penalty.

questions.question: The independent variable is the variable measured in a study.
questions.explanation: The IV is MANIPULATED by the researcher. The DV (dependent variable) is MEASURED.

questions.question: Operationalisation means defining variables in a measurable way.
questions.explanation: Variables must be operationalised to make them testable — e.g. 'memory' measured as 'number of words recalled'.

questions.question: Laboratory experiments have high ecological validity.
questions.explanation: Lab experiments have high INTERNAL validity (control) but low ECOLOGICAL validity — the artificial setting lacks mundane realism.

questions.question: A correlation can establish cause and effect.
questions.explanation: Correlation shows a relationship but cannot establish causation — a third variable may explain both.

questions.question: Field experiments take place in natural settings.
questions.explanation: Field experiments manipulate the IV in real-world settings — more ecological validity than lab experiments.

questions.question: Participants must be fully debriefed after a study involving deception.
questions.explanation: Debriefing informs participants of the study's true purpose and allows them to withdraw their data.

questions.question: In a natural experiment, the researcher manipulates the IV.
questions.explanation: In a natural experiment, the IV occurs naturally — the researcher does not control it.

questions.question: Extraneous variables must be controlled to maintain internal validity.
questions.explanation: Uncontrolled extraneous variables can confound results — making it impossible to conclude the IV caused changes in the DV.

questions.question: Informed consent means participants must know the full hypothesis before taking part.
questions.explanation: Informed consent means participants are given enough information to decide whether to take part — but full hypotheses are not always disclosed (to avoid demand characteristics).

questions.question: Higher ecological validity means findings are more generalisable to real life.
questions.explanation: Ecological validity refers to how well findings apply to real-world settings — field experiments have higher ecological validity than lab experiments.

questions.question: A ________ is a clear, testable prediction of what will happen in a study.
questions.explanation: Hypotheses must be operationalised — variables defined in measurable terms.

questions.question: The ________ variable is manipulated by the researcher.
questions.explanation: The IV is what is changed. The DV is what is measured.

questions.question: The ________ variable is measured by the researcher — affected by the IV.
questions.explanation: The DV shows the effect of the IV manipulation.

questions.question: ________ is defining variables in a measurable way.
questions.explanation: E.g. 'anxiety' operationalised as 'score on a validated anxiety questionnaire'.

questions.question: Lab experiments have high ________ validity but low ecological validity.
questions.explanation: Tight control in labs allows cause-and-effect conclusions but limits real-world generalisability.

questions.question: A ________ shows a relationship between two variables but not causation.
questions.explanation: Correlation coefficient ranges from −1 (perfect negative) to +1 (perfect positive).

questions.question: ________ experiments take place in natural settings with the IV manipulated.
questions.explanation: Higher ecological validity than labs — but less control over extraneous variables.

questions.question: ________ experiments use naturally occurring IV changes — researcher does not manipulate.
questions.explanation: Natural experiments have high ecological validity but cannot establish causation.

questions.question: ________ variables must be controlled to prevent them confounding results.
questions.explanation: If extraneous variables are not controlled, they become confounding variables.

questions.question: The right to ________ means participants can leave a study at any time without penalty.
questions.explanation: This is a key ethical guideline protecting participant autonomy.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: the variable manipulated by the researcher
the variable measured — affected by the IV
defining variables in measurable terms
controlled setting — high internal, low ecological validity
measures relationship between variables — not causation
